C1 Atomic Structure and the Periodic Tab.question: State the three subatomic particles found in an atom. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Proton (1 mark)
Neutron (1 mark)
Electron (1 mark)

C1 Atomic Structure and the Periodic Tab.question: Describe the charge of a proton and an electron. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Proton has a positive charge (1 mark)
Electron has a negative charge (1 mark)

C1 Atomic Structure and the Periodic Tab.question: Explain what an isotope is. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Atoms of the same element (1 mark)
With different numbers of neutrons (1 mark)

C1 Atomic Structure and the Periodic Tab.question: Describe the difference between an element and a compound. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: An element contains only one type of atom (1 mark)
A compound contains two or more elements chemically bonded together (1 mark)

C1 Atomic Structure and the Periodic Tab.question: State what a mixture is. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: Two or more substances not chemically bonded together (1 mark)

C1 Atomic Structure and the Periodic Tab.question: Describe how filtration separates a mixture. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: An insoluble solid is trapped by the filter paper (1 mark)
The liquid / filtrate passes through (1 mark)

C1 Atomic Structure and the Periodic Tab.question: Explain how simple distillation works. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: The solution is heated (1 mark)
The solvent evaporates / boils first (1 mark)
The vapour is condensed and collected (1 mark)

C1 Atomic Structure and the Periodic Tab.question: Describe how paper chromatography can separate inks. [4 marks]
mark_scheme: Different substances have different solubilities in the solvent (1 mark)
They move at different speeds (1 mark)
So they travel different distances up the paper (1 mark)
This produces separate spots (1 mark)

C2 Bonding, Structure, and the Propertie.question: Explain how an ionic bond forms. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Electrons are transferred between atoms (1 mark)
This forms oppositely charged ions (1 mark)
These ions are held together by strong electrostatic attraction (1 mark)

C2 Bonding, Structure, and the Propertie.question: Describe how a covalent bond forms. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Atoms share pairs of electrons (1 mark)
So each atom gains a full outer shell (1 mark)

C2 Bonding, Structure, and the Propertie.question: State one property of ionic compounds. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: Any one from high melting point / conduct electricity when molten or in solution / brittle (1 mark)

C2 Bonding, Structure, and the Propertie.question: Explain why graphite conducts electricity. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Graphite has delocalised electrons (1 mark)
These electrons can move through the structure and carry charge (1 mark)

C2 Bonding, Structure, and the Propertie.question: Describe one difference between diamond and graphite. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Diamond has each carbon atom bonded to four others whereas graphite has each carbon atom bonded to three others (1 mark)
Diamond is hard and does not conduct electricity whereas graphite is soft and conducts (1 mark)

C2 Bonding, Structure, and the Propertie.question: State what an alloy is. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: A mixture of a metal with one or more other elements (1 mark)

C2 Bonding, Structure, and the Propertie.question: Explain why alloys are harder than pure metals. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Atoms in an alloy are different sizes (1 mark)
This distorts the layers (1 mark)
So the layers cannot slide over each other as easily (1 mark)

C2 Bonding, Structure, and the Propertie.question: Describe the structure and one use of graphene. [4 marks]
mark_scheme: Single layer of carbon atoms (1 mark)
Atoms arranged in hexagons (1 mark)
Strong / conducts electricity (1 mark)
Used in electronics / composites / sensors (1 mark)

C3 Quantitative Chemistry.question: State why chemical equations must be balanced. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: To show the same number of each type of atom on both sides (1 mark)

C3 Quantitative Chemistry.question: Calculate the relative formula mass of H2O. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: 2 × 1 for hydrogen (1 mark)
16 for oxygen giving 18 (1 mark)

C3 Quantitative Chemistry.question: State what one mole of a substance means. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: The amount of substance containing 6.02 × 10^23 particles (1 mark)

C3 Quantitative Chemistry.question: Explain what percentage yield measures. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Compares the actual amount of product made with the theoretical amount (1 mark)
Shows how much product was obtained from the reaction (1 mark)

C3 Quantitative Chemistry.question: State what is meant by atom economy. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: A measure of how much of the reactants become useful products (1 mark)
Shown as a percentage (1 mark)

C3 Quantitative Chemistry.question: Describe what is meant by concentration. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: The amount of solute dissolved in a given volume of solution (1 mark)

C3 Quantitative Chemistry.question: Explain why atom economy is important in industry. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: A higher atom economy means more of the reactants become useful product (1 mark)
Less waste is produced (1 mark)
This reduces costs / environmental impact (1 mark)

C3 Quantitative Chemistry.question: Describe how a titration is used to find the volume of acid needed to neutralise an alkali. [4 marks]
mark_scheme: Measure a fixed volume of alkali into a flask (1 mark)
Add indicator (1 mark)
Add acid from a burette until the end point is reached (1 mark)
Record the volume of acid used (1 mark)

C4 Chemical Changes.question: State what happens in an oxidation reaction. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: Gain of oxygen / loss of electrons (1 mark)

C4 Chemical Changes.question: State what happens in a reduction reaction. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: Loss of oxygen / gain of electrons (1 mark)

C4 Chemical Changes.question: Describe what happens when an acid reacts with an alkali. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Neutralisation occurs (1 mark)
A salt and water are produced (1 mark)

C4 Chemical Changes.question: Explain why magnesium reacts more vigorously with acid than copper. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Magnesium is higher in the reactivity series (1 mark)
So it loses electrons / reacts more easily (1 mark)

C4 Chemical Changes.question: State the products formed when copper chloride solution is electrolysed using inert electrodes. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Copper (1 mark)
Chlorine (1 mark)

C4 Chemical Changes.question: Describe what happens at the negative electrode during electrolysis. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Positive ions gain electrons (1 mark)
Reduction occurs (1 mark)

C4 Chemical Changes.question: Explain how displacement reactions show the reactivity of metals. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: A more reactive metal displaces a less reactive metal from its compound (1 mark)
A less reactive metal cannot displace a more reactive metal (1 mark)
So the reaction shows which metal is more reactive (1 mark)

C4 Chemical Changes.question: Describe how to make a pure dry salt from an acid and an insoluble base. [4 marks]
mark_scheme: Add excess insoluble base to the acid until no more reacts (1 mark)
Filter to remove excess solid (1 mark)
Heat the filtrate to evaporate some water (1 mark)
Allow crystals to form and dry them (1 mark)

C5 Energy Changes.question: State what is meant by an exothermic reaction. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: A reaction that transfers energy to the surroundings (1 mark)

C5 Energy Changes.question: State what is meant by an endothermic reaction. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: A reaction that takes in energy from the surroundings (1 mark)

C5 Energy Changes.question: Describe one energy change in an exothermic reaction. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Energy is released to the surroundings (1 mark)
Temperature of the surroundings increases (1 mark)

C5 Energy Changes.question: Describe one energy change in an endothermic reaction. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Energy is taken in from the surroundings (1 mark)
Temperature of the surroundings decreases (1 mark)

C5 Energy Changes.question: Explain what is shown in a reaction profile diagram. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: The energy changes during a reaction (1 mark)
The activation energy / overall energy change can be shown (1 mark)

C5 Energy Changes.question: State what activation energy is. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: The minimum amount of energy particles must have to react (1 mark)

C5 Energy Changes.question: Explain why hand warmers are useful examples of exothermic reactions. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: They release energy to the surroundings (1 mark)
This increases temperature (1 mark)
So they warm the hands (1 mark)

C5 Energy Changes.question: Explain why sports injury packs often use endothermic reactions. [4 marks]
mark_scheme: The reaction takes in energy from the surroundings (1 mark)
Energy is taken from the injured area (1 mark)
This lowers the temperature (1 mark)
Cooling can reduce pain / swelling (1 mark)

C6 The Rate and Extent of Chemical Chang.question: State what is meant by the rate of reaction. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: How fast reactants are changed into products (1 mark)

C6 The Rate and Extent of Chemical Chang.question: Explain collision theory. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Particles must collide to react (1 mark)
Only collisions with enough energy lead to a reaction (1 mark)

C6 The Rate and Extent of Chemical Chang.question: Describe how increasing temperature affects the rate of reaction. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Particles gain kinetic energy (1 mark)
They move faster and collide more often (1 mark)
More collisions have energy greater than the activation energy (1 mark)

C6 The Rate and Extent of Chemical Chang.question: Explain why a catalyst speeds up a reaction. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: It provides an alternative reaction pathway (1 mark)
With a lower activation energy (1 mark)

C6 The Rate and Extent of Chemical Chang.question: State what is meant by a reversible reaction. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: A reaction in which the products can react to form the original reactants (1 mark)

C6 The Rate and Extent of Chemical Chang.question: Describe dynamic equilibrium. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Forward and reverse reactions continue (1 mark)
At the same rate (1 mark)

C6 The Rate and Extent of Chemical Chang.question: Explain the difference between exothermic and endothermic reactions. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Exothermic reactions transfer energy to the surroundings (1 mark)
Endothermic reactions take in energy from the surroundings (1 mark)
Temperature increases in exothermic / decreases in endothermic reactions (1 mark)

C6 The Rate and Extent of Chemical Chang.question: Describe how increasing concentration can speed up a reaction between a solution and a solid. [4 marks]
mark_scheme: There are more reacting particles in a given volume (1 mark)
So collisions happen more often (1 mark)
This increases the frequency of successful collisions (1 mark)
So the rate of reaction increases (1 mark)

C7 Organic Chemistry.question: State what crude oil is. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: A mixture of hydrocarbons (1 mark)

C7 Organic Chemistry.question: State what a hydrocarbon is. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: A compound made of hydrogen and carbon only (1 mark)

C7 Organic Chemistry.question: Describe how fractional distillation separates crude oil. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Crude oil is heated so that most of it vaporises (1 mark)
The vapours rise through the fractionating column (1 mark)
Different fractions condense at different temperatures / boiling points (1 mark)

C7 Organic Chemistry.question: State the general formula for alkanes. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: CnH2n+2 (1 mark)

C7 Organic Chemistry.question: Explain why alkanes are described as saturated. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: They contain only single carbon-carbon bonds (1 mark)

C7 Organic Chemistry.question: Describe what happens during cracking. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Long-chain hydrocarbons are broken down (1 mark)
Into smaller alkanes and alkenes (1 mark)

C7 Organic Chemistry.question: Explain why cracking is useful. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: Produces more useful smaller molecules (1 mark)
Produces alkenes used to make polymers (1 mark)
Produces fuels in greater demand such as petrol (1 mark)

C7 Organic Chemistry.question: Describe one disadvantage of burning hydrocarbon fuels. [4 marks]
mark_scheme: Carbon dioxide is produced (1 mark)
This contributes to climate change (1 mark)
If combustion is incomplete carbon monoxide may be produced (1 mark)
This is toxic / harmful to health (1 mark)

C8 Chemical Analysis.question: State what pH tells you about a solution. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: How acidic or alkaline the solution is (1 mark)

C8 Chemical Analysis.question: Describe the colour of universal indicator in a strong acid. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: Red (1 mark)

C8 Chemical Analysis.question: State the test result for carbonate ions. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Add dilute acid / effervescence observed (1 mark)
Carbon dioxide turns limewater milky (1 mark)

C8 Chemical Analysis.question: Describe the flame test result for sodium ions. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: Yellow / orange-yellow flame (1 mark)

C8 Chemical Analysis.question: Explain why pure substances have a sharp melting point. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: A pure substance has a fixed composition (1 mark)
So it melts at one specific temperature (1 mark)

C8 Chemical Analysis.question: Describe how chromatography can be used to test if a sample is pure. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: A pure substance produces one spot (1 mark)
A mixture produces more than one spot (1 mark)

C8 Chemical Analysis.question: Explain why solvent fronts are marked immediately in chromatography. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Because the solvent evaporates (1 mark)
So the solvent front would no longer be visible (1 mark)

C8 Chemical Analysis.question: Describe how gas tests identify oxygen and hydrogen. [4 marks]
mark_scheme: Oxygen relights a glowing splint (1 mark)
Hydrogen burns with a squeaky pop (1 mark)
The tests are carried out by holding a lit / glowing splint near the gas (1 mark)
The observations identify the gases (1 mark)

C9 Chemistry of the Atmosphere.question: State the approximate percentage of nitrogen in the Earth's atmosphere. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: About 78% (1 mark)

C9 Chemistry of the Atmosphere.question: State the approximate percentage of oxygen in the Earth's atmosphere. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: About 21% (1 mark)

C9 Chemistry of the Atmosphere.question: Describe how carbon dioxide is removed from the atmosphere. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Taken in by plants during photosynthesis (1 mark)
Dissolves in the oceans / forms carbonate rocks (1 mark)

C9 Chemistry of the Atmosphere.question: Explain how human activity has increased the amount of carbon dioxide in the atmosphere. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Burning fossil fuels releases carbon dioxide (1 mark)
Deforestation reduces the amount removed by photosynthesis (1 mark)

C9 Chemistry of the Atmosphere.question: State one greenhouse gas. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: Any one from carbon dioxide / methane / water vapour (1 mark)

C9 Chemistry of the Atmosphere.question: Explain how greenhouse gases cause global warming. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: They absorb infrared radiation emitted from the Earth's surface (1 mark)
This traps energy in the atmosphere (1 mark)
So the average temperature of the Earth increases (1 mark)

C9 Chemistry of the Atmosphere.question: Describe one effect of global climate change. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Rising sea levels / changes in rainfall / loss of habitats / changes in distribution of species (1 mark)
Linked consequence (1 mark)

C9 Chemistry of the Atmosphere.question: Explain one way scientists think the early atmosphere changed over time. [4 marks]
mark_scheme: Early atmosphere had a high concentration of carbon dioxide (1 mark)
Volcanoes released gases into the atmosphere (1 mark)
Carbon dioxide decreased as oceans formed / photosynthetic organisms evolved (1 mark)
Oxygen increased due to photosynthesis by algae / plants (1 mark)

C10 Using Resources.question: State what is meant by a finite resource. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: A resource that is being used up faster than it is replaced / will run out (1 mark)

C10 Using Resources.question: State one reason why potable water is important. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: It is safe for drinking (1 mark)

C10 Using Resources.question: Describe one method used to produce potable water from fresh water. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Filtration removes solids (1 mark)
Sterilisation kills microorganisms (1 mark)

C10 Using Resources.question: Explain why desalination is used in some places. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Fresh water is limited / there is a shortage of fresh water (1 mark)
Desalination removes salt from sea water to make it safe to drink (1 mark)

C10 Using Resources.question: State what corrosion is. [1 mark]
mark_scheme: The destruction of materials by chemical reactions with the environment (1 mark)

C10 Using Resources.question: Describe what is needed for iron to rust. [2 marks]
mark_scheme: Oxygen (1 mark)
Water (1 mark)

C10 Using Resources.question: Explain how galvanising prevents rusting. [3 marks]
mark_scheme: The iron is coated with zinc (1 mark)
This acts as a barrier to oxygen and water (1 mark)
Zinc is more reactive so it provides sacrificial protection if the coating is damaged (1 mark)

C10 Using Resources.question: Describe one benefit of recycling metals. [4 marks]
mark_scheme: Less energy is needed than extracting metals from ores (1 mark)
Conserves finite resources (1 mark)
Reduces landfill waste (1 mark)
Reduces environmental damage from mining (1 mark)
